digital_innovation: Sensor-based monitoring of pedestrian flows and dwell times was used to assess impact of the transformation on space usage and stay-duration, supporting data-driven decisions for the permanent redesign.
c5_impacts:
Usage increase
During the temporary transformation, sensors rec
orded an average >27,000 daily presences, with a peak of ~36,000
Policy impact
The pilot triggered the Municipal Board’s decision to permanently pedestrianise Piazza Rossini, reallocating former parking and loading areas to surrounding streets and cycling facilities.
Social participation and community building
The project emerged from the U-Lab process involving 250+ participants, strengthening local stewardship and shared decision-making in the university district
sources:
Cultural Heritage in Action. (2021). A new life for Piazza Rossini—From a parking lot to a pedestrian green space (Case study, Bologna). (Accessed on November 9, 2025).  Cultural Heritage In Action
Boulanger, S. O. M., Boeri, A., et al. (2020). Data Evidence-Based Transformative Actions in Historic Urban Context—The Bologna University Area Case Study. Smart Cities, 3(4), 1346–1369. (Accessed on November 9, 2025).

title: Sankt Kjelds Plads & Bryggervangen
designer: SLA Architects
promoter: City of Copenhagen – Copenhagen Climate Adaptation Plan
description: First climate-resilient neighborhood, transforming a major traffic junction and its surrounding streets into a lush, multi-functional urban park. The design integrates nature-based stormwater management, biodiversity restoration, and social space creation. The area now functions as a living, cooling, and adaptive landscape that enhances urban life while mitigating climate risks.
physical_innovation: The project depaves large asphalted surfaces and introduces an integrated blue-green infrastructure system capable of collecting, filtering, and storing stormwater. The topography was redesigned to channel water naturally into planted basins and permeable surfaces, increasing infiltration and evapotranspiration. Over 600 trees and 30,000 plants were introduced, enabling the system to retain and delay up to 24,000 m³ of stormwater during cloudbursts.
social_innovation: Public participation guided the redesign of the area, prioritizing spaces for community use, accessibility, and safety. The square and streets were reimagined as inclusive environments that encourage social interaction, play, and well-being. The transformation also changed local perceptions of green infrastructure—from technical installations to everyday public spaces that improve quality of life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Flood and stormwater resilience
The blue-green retrofit manages a significant share of rainfall on the surface via rain gardens, detention spaces and cloudburst routes, relieving sewers and lowering flood risk at Østerbro. 
Biodiversity and urban cooling
Replacing asphalt with 586 trees, perennial beds and wild grass creates habitat connectivity and shade, boosting evapotranspiration and thermal comfort while enriching local ecology. 
Public realm and health
About 9,000 m² of asphalt was removed to make walkable, bike-friendly, shaded spaces. Reported outcomes include reduced noise and air pollution and improved everyday livability for residents.
sources: SLA Architects. (2019). Sankt Kjelds Square & Bryggervangen. (2015–2019; 34,900 m²; client: City of Copenhagen). Retrieved from SLA website. (Accessed on November 6, 2025)

title: Hamamyolu Urban Deck
designer: Yazgan Landscape Architecture
promoter: Odunpazarı Municipality
description: Spanning 26,088 m² in the former centre of Eskişehir, the intervention reinvents the pedestrian axis of Hamamyolu Street, reconnecting the city’s north and south, creating a continuous pedestrian route of 1.2 km embedded with a glass-and-LED line (22,000 local handmade glass pieces), planting 50 new lindens plus 3,877 ivies and 2,622 shrubs to form a green belt, and transforming the street into a vibrant public space that supports both tourism and daily life
physical_innovation: The project replaces a vehicle-dominated axis with a layered structure: surfaces are over-decked (‘urban deck’), a pedestrian bridge passes over the former road, a continuous LED and handmade glass inlay marks the path, existing and new trees create a green corridor, and integrated play and water elements are embedded into the deck, thereby merging infrastructure, culture, green and blue systems
social_innovation: By reconnecting north and south parts of the city via a pedestrian axis and creating a highly useable public zone with cafés, amphitheatres, playgrounds and seating (free seating capacity increased from 1,000 to 5,000) the project transformed the space into a social magnet that supports cultural identity, craft economy (glass production), community gathering and inter-age usage.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Pedestrian connectivity
The project transformed 26,088 m² of road-dominated public space into a continuous pedestrian axis over 1.2 km, reconnecting previously fragmented areas of Eskişehir’s historic centre and revitalising commercial and cultural activities along the route.
Microclimate
Three pools — the Stone Pool, Steam Pool, and Pigeon Pool — introduce evaporative cooling and enhance the sensory quality of the public realm, mitigating the heat stress
Social inclusion and accessibility
The new pedestrian deck increased seating capacity from ~1,000 to 5,000 people, enabling multigenerational use, open-air cultural events, and accessible leisure for children, elderly, and visitors
Urban safety and liveability
Conversion from vehicular traffic to pedestrian use reduced traffic hazards and noise, enhancing perceived and actual safety.
sources:
Yazgan Landscape Architecture. (2019, July 1). Hamamyolu Urban Deck by Yazgan. Retrieved from https://landezine.com/hamamyolu-urban-deck-by-yazgan/
(Accessed on November 9, 2025)

title: Ballerup Boulevard
designer: Marianne Levinsen Landskab
promoter: Municipality of Ballerup
description: The boulevard was transformed from a four-lane high-traffic road into a two-lane green boulevard. The former road surface was narrowed and repurposed into green planting beds, tree avenues, storm-water infiltration areas and wide pedestrian/cycle paths, thus converting a barrier into a connective, human-scale green corridor.
physical_innovation: The project physically retreated the vehicular footprint by reducing from four to two lanes, and established extensive planting beds, tree groves and storm-water infiltration strips along the sidewalks and cycle tracks.
social_innovation: By shifting the emphasis from cars to people and green space, the boulevard now offers wide pedestrian and cycle zones, informal recreation areas, and a more favourable environment for social interaction and neighbourhood connectivity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Social and environmental benefit
Improved air quality, noise reduction, and higher social use of public realm recorded post-completion
Storm-water management
Infiltration trenches and permeable surfaces handle runoff locally, reducing sewer load.
Reduction of vehicular area
Road lanes reduced from four to two, cutting paved surface by approximately 50 %.

title: Little C
designer: CULD (Juurlink + Geluk, in collaboration with INBO Architects and Office Winhov)
promoter: Municipality of Rotterdam + ERA Contour
description: Little C transforms a neglected infrastructural void between highways and canals near Coolhaven into a vibrant mixed-use neighbourhood of 15 compact blocks. Combining brick architecture, pedestrian bridges, lush courtyards, green façades, and roof gardens, the project reconnects fragmented urban fabrics, introduces sustainable water management, and creates new public and semi-public spaces.
physical_innovation: The project overlays dense urban form with multilayered green infrastructure: green roofs, vertical planting, water-retention decks beneath pavements, and new micro-courtyards create a resilient “city within the city.” It merges architectural density with permeability and ecosystem functions.
social_innovation: Little C fosters social connection through shared gardens, semi-public courtyards, pedestrian bridges, and communal roof terraces. The design enables encounters and inclusion within a high-density context, redefining liveability in compact urban quarters.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
sources:
Juurlink + Geluk / CULD. (2021). Little C by Juurlink + Geluk / CULD Rotterdam. Retrieved from https://landezine.com/little-c-by-juurlink-geluk-culd-rotterdam/
(Accessed on November 9, 2025)

title: Girondins Development Area (ZAC des Girondins)
designer: BASE Landscape Architecture, with EGIS VRD, Transitec, Les Éclaireurs
promoter: SERL – Société d’Équipement du Rhône et de Lyon
description: The area is a 17.5 ha urban regeneration project in Lyon’s Gerland district. The masterplan transforms an industrial site into a mixed-use neighbourhood structured by 2 axes: the Allée Fontenay (N-S green boulevard) and Rue des Girondins (E-W avenue). The design integrates green-blue infrastructure, sustainable drainage systems (SUDS), and inclusive public spaces, turning the site into a climate-resilient, vibrant quarter. The project demonstrates systemic integration of urban morphology, ecological infrastructure, and climate adaptation. The “green and blue backbone” operates as a multifunctional spine—both a hydrological system and a bioclimatic corridor.
physical_innovation: The project transforms a former industrial brownfield into a porous and climate-responsive urban fabric. A continuous green-blue infrastructure — including rain gardens, vegetated swales, permeable paving, and tree-lined boulevards — manages stormwater, reduces heat, and enhances biodiversity. The Allée Fontenay acts as a linear climatic corridor, integrating mobility, shade, and ecological continuity within the dense urban grid. Landscape and infrastructure merge into a multifunctional system that turns the public realm into an adaptive device for climate resilience.
social_innovation: The masterplan promotes social inclusion and everyday interaction through accessible, shaded public spaces and shared courtyards. The layout encourages proximity, community life, and mixed uses along green pedestrian routes. Co-design activities and public consultations coordinated by SERL involved citizens and local associations in defining uses and management, strengthening civic ownership and social cohesion.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Ecological connectivity
The Allée Fontenay creates a continuous 1 km green corridor linking Gerland’s fragmented ecological systems.
Surface permeability and stormwater management
Permeable and vegetated areas increased by more than 40 % compared to pre-development. SUDS and vegetated ditches reduce runoff and flooding risk.
Biodiversity
Hundreds of trees and native species introduced, providing new habitats for urban fauna.
Thermal comfort improvement
Shaded and ventilated corridors mitigate heat stress during summer peaks
Social inclusion
Public spaces promote interaction and accessibility for all age groups.
sources:
Landezine. (2023). Girondins Development Area – Lyon by BASE. Retrieved from https://landezine.com/girondins-development-area-lyon-by-base/
(Accessed on November 9, 2025)

Les Éclaireurs. (2020). ZAC des Girondins, Lyon. Retrieved from https://www.leseclaireurs.net/projects/girondins-district-lyon
 (Accessed on November 9, 2025)

title: Freiburger Bächle
year: Origin 12th–13th century; restored and enhanced over time (most recent 19th–21st century)
designer: Historical system
promoter: City of Freiburg
description: The Bächle are narrow open-water channels running along the streets of Freiburg's old town. Fed by the Dreisam River, they form a continuous blue infrastructure originally used for irrigation, firefighting and drainage. Today they serve as a cultural symbol, micro-climatic cooling system and playful urban amenity, fostering social interaction and comfort in the historic centre.
physical_innovation: Reactivation of an historical open-water network as urban cooling infrastructure. The Bächle channel fresh river water through the city centre, improving microclimate, reducing urban heat and enhancing sensory quality. Their continuous, low-depth design integrates seamlessly with narrow medieval streets.
social_innovation: The Bächle act as informal social infrastructure: children play with water, residents and tourists interact along the canals, and the channels strengthen place identity. Their accessibility and symbolic value support community life and cultural heritage.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Urban cooling and pedestrian comfort
The flowing water creates evaporative cooling, reducing perceived temperature during summer peaks. Bächle improve the sensory experience of streets and attract foot traffic, supporting local commerce.

Public space activation
Children and families use Bächle as playful micro-places, enhancing vibrancy and dwell time in the historic centre.

Cultural identity reinforcement
he canals strengthen local identity and heritage, contributing to Freiburg’s branding and tourism appeal.

Blue infrastructure resilience
Water circulation from the Dreisam provides continuous flushing and supports runoff management.
sources:
Freiburg bächle. (n.d.). Academic Dictionaries and Encyclopedias.https://en-academic.com/dic.nsf/enwiki/10097675
Çatal, S. (2024). Water as a Sustainable Infrastructure and Cultural Symbol of City: Bächle in Freiburg im Breisgau.
Alto Adige Innovazione. (2023). I Bächle, i canaletti di Friburgo.
City of Freiburg Tourism Office (2024). Freiburger Bächle.

title: Calle María Díaz de Haro – Climate-Adapted Green Corridor
designer: Bilbao City / Municipal Urban Planning Department
promoter: City of Bilbao (Ayuntamiento de Bilbao)
description: Transformation of a 700 m traffic-dominated street into a green and permeable pedestrian-oriented corridor. The project reduces car lanes, removes parking, widens sidewalks, adds trees and planting strips, and integrates permeable surfaces to mitigate heat and manage stormwater. It creates a distributed climatic refuge and improves ecological continuity between Casilda Park and Ametzola.
physical_innovation: Conversion of a three-lane road into a permeable, shaded green corridor. Two traffic lanes and parking are replaced by wide sidewalks, planting beds, and continuous tree rows. Permeable pavements and vegetated strips control runoff and mitigate urban heat. The works redesigned over 4,500 m² of pedestrian space with permeable surfaces, improved infiltration, and lowered surface temperatures. A continuous corridor of deciduous, perennial, native species enhances shade, evapotranspiration cooling, and ecological continuity. The intervention also added 1,300 m² of retention areas and removed more than 2,800 m² of asphalt.
social_innovation: The corridor becomes a comfortable public space supporting walkability, rest areas, social interaction, and everyday outdoor life. It strengthens pedestrian connections between key neighbourhood facilities and parks.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
b1_physical: Existing large tree or plant roots
c5_impacts:
Increase in permeable pedestrian surface
The transformation of the street created over 4,500 m² of new pedestrian areas built with permeable materials. This expansion reduces surface temperatures, supports natural infiltration, and improves the thermal comfort of daily walking routes.
Stormwater retention capacity added
Through the introduction of rain gardens, green strips, and bioretentive soil sections, the project integrated approximately 1,300 m² of stormwater retention zones, significantly improving local flood resilience during heavy rainfall events.
Reduction of impermeable road surface and traffic volumes
By removing more than 2,800 m² of asphalt roadway, the project decreased car lanes and eliminated parking areas, resulting in a measurable reduction in vehicular traffic volumes, lower emissions, and improved air quality along the corridor.
sources:
European Environment Agency (2024). Transforming a busy road into a public open green space in Bilbao. Climate-ADAPT.
Cadenaser (2025). María Díaz de Haro se transforma: así quedará el nuevo corredor verde de Bilbao.
City of Bilbao (2024). Bilbao Green Corridors Programme.

title: Zollhallen Plaza
designer: Henning Larsen Studio (Landscape Architecture)
promoter: City of Freiburg
description: A 5,600 m² post-industrial plaza adjacent to the historic customs hall, redesigned with water-sensitive urban design. The plaza is disconnected from the conventional sewer system, using infiltration planters and subsurface gravel trenches with filter media to reduce hydraulic load. It incorporates indented surface zones designed as temporary flood zones and recharges groundwater rather than draining rainwater to the sewer.
physical_innovation: The design establishes a plaza that performs as a multifunctional, water-resilient platform. Rainwater is managed on-site via permeable pavers, infiltration planters, gravel trenches and dedicated flood-zones – eliminating direct connection to the sewer and recharging groundwater. The use of recycled hardscape material from the old railyard emphasises resource reuse and harmonises with the historic industrial context.
social_innovation: The transformation turns a former industrial yard into a public square that supports everyday use, social gathering and environmental awareness. By embedding visible water-management infrastructure (planters, flood zones) into a comfortable urban plaza with shade trees (e.g., cherry trees) and seating, the project fosters a public realm that educates, activates and connects citizens with site-specific ecological processes.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Visible water-sensitive design as public awareness tool
By integrating infiltration planters, permeable surfaces and designated flood zones directly into the plaza’s layout, the project makes water management processes visible to all users. This strengthens public awareness of climate adaptation and turns the square into a daily learning environment where hydrological cycles become tangible.
Circular use of materials from the former railyard
The reuse of hardscape materials salvaged from the old industrial site demonstrates a concrete application of circular economy principles. The continuity in textures and materials preserves the site’s memory while reducing the environmental footprint traditionally associated with plaza construction.
Activation of a former industrial void as a social space
The transformation of an abandoned railyard surface into a multifunctional plaza generates new opportunities for gathering, leisure and open-air activities. Shaded seating, cherry trees and accessible pathways encourage everyday use, supporting community life and soft mobility within the district.
sources:
Henning Larsen Studio. (2015, April 2). Zollhallen Plaza by Henning Larsen. Retrieved from https://landezine.com/flood-zone-on-public-plaza-design-by-henning-larsen/
Urban Resilience Thesis. (2025). Case study – Zollhallen Plaza, Freiburg. Retrieved from webthesis.biblio.polito.it/34542/

title: Brantstraße – Animal-Aided Design
designer: Landscape architect Thomas E. Hauck & ecologist Wolfgang Weisser (AAD method)
promoter: Bavarian State Ministry for the Environment and Consumer Protection; Munich Housing D.nt
description: The Brantstraße project in Munich is the world’s first built example of Animal-Aided Design (AAD). It integrates habitat features for selected target species into the design of residential outdoor spaces. Nesting structures, feeding habitats, micro-wetlands, flowering meadows and species-specific landscape elements were implemented to enable coexistence between humans and urban fauna. The project transforms an ordinary housing area into a biodiversity-supporting environment embedded within daily life.
physical_innovation: Integration of species-specific micro-habitats into residential outdoor spaces, including nesting boxes, structured vegetation layers, continuous flowering meadows and micro-water elements. The design ensures essential life-cycle requirements (breeding, feeding, shelter) for target species directly within the built environment.
social_innovation: The project raises ecological awareness among residents by embedding fauna-supportive features in everyday spaces. Educational signage and community involvement help residents understand how their environment coexists with birds, insects and hedgehogs, strengthening environmental stewardship.
digital_innovation: Digital ecological tools and AAD planning software were used to map life-cycle needs of animals, matching each need with a corresponding landscape or architectural intervention.
b1_physical: Existing large tree or plant roots
c5_impacts:
Biodiversity integrated into everyday living
By embedding nesting boxes, flowering meadows and feeding habitats into the housing courtyard, the project enables residents to live daily alongside birds, insects and small mammals. This fosters ecological awareness and makes biodiversity a tangible part of neighbourhood life.
Life-cycle habitats designed for target species
Animal-Aided Design identifies the full life-cycle needs of selected species and translates them into concrete spatial interventions. Brantstraße demonstrates how urban development can provide breeding, shelter and feeding habitats within a residential setting.
Educational value for residents and planners
Interpretive elements and the visibility of habitat structures help residents understand ecological processes. The project also serves as a reference model for urban planners, showing how fauna-oriented design can be integrated into regulatory planning and housing development.
d4_roof_facade: Climbing structures on walls or nets
sources:
Animal-Aided Design. (n.d.). Animal-Aided Design in the living environment – A project from Munich
Hauck, T. E., & Weisser, W. (2018). Animal-Aided Design in the living environment – Brantstraße, Munich. Animal-Aided Design.

title: Kettingplein
designer: De Urbanisten together with OMGEVING
promoter: City of Ghent
description: The Kettingplein project transforms an under-used small square in the heart of a residential neighbourhood in Ghent into a “climate square” with green raingarden features, removal of car-traffic, and redesigned surfaces. Rainwater from adjacent roads and roofs is directed into the new square, stored and infiltrated (approx. 100 m³ capacity) via bowl-shaped raingarden basins and underground storage. The space is designed as a car-free, shaded, green room for the neighbourhood.
physical_innovation: The intervention reduces hard paved surfaces significantly, replaces them with green spaces and gentle depressions to create a raingarden that stores and infiltrates storm-water up to approx. 100 m³. The square is freed from cars and designed as a shared pedestrian/cyclist zone with seating, shade trees and organic forms
social_innovation: The redesign supports neighbourhood life by creating a comfortable and accessible public space where residents can meet and linger. A participatory process included local residents to shape the design, thereby reinforcing ownership and using the space as a “living room” for the communit
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Local infiltration and water buffering capacity
The redesigned square handles a total storage volume of around 100 m³ of rainwater from adjacent roads and roofs, channelled into depressions and underground storage, significantly reducing surface runoff and improving local storm resilience. 
Improved neighbourhood comfort and greenery
By replacing large areas of impermeable paving with green planting beds, shade trees and a calm pedestrian-friendly layout, the project creates a comfortable “living room” in the neighbourhood, supporting social use, outdoor activities and slower mobility.
Enhanced community involvement and engagement
The design process included participatory workshops with around 50 local residents in 2017, allowing their input on features such as slope gradients, seating design and access for older users and those with limited mobility, strengthening local ownership of the public space.
sources:
“Kettingplein, Ghent – DE Urbanisten.” DE Urbanisten. Retrieved from https://www.urbanisten.nl/work/kettingplein
Stad Gent. (2017, March 31). Eerste participatiemoment – Kettingplein. Retrieved from https://stad.gent/…/DO_Kettingplein_Participatiemoment.pdf

title: Ciudad Dulce
designer: Municipality of Curridabat + interdisciplinary team (ecologists, urban designers)
promoter: Municipality of Curridabat
description: Ciudad Dulce is a city-wide ecological and social regeneration programme that reconceives Curridabat as a multispecies city. Through pollinator corridors, neighbourhood gardens, permeable streets, composting systems and urban food production, it embeds biodiversity into everyday life. The project reframes nature as civic infrastructure and recognises non-human species as urban inhabitants, reshaping the environmental and social fabric of the city.
physical_innovation: The programme delivers a distributed system of ecological micro-interventions—pollinator gardens, tree-lined corridors, micro-wetlands, permeable sidewalks and community orchards—integrated directly into residential streets, schools and public spaces. This granular strategy improves shade, soil permeability, microclimate and habitat diversity throughout the urban fabric.
social_innovation: Ciudad Dulce pioneers a multispecies citizenship model by recognising pollinators and urban fauna as members of the community. Social innovation emerges through shared gardening, community composting, ecological education, neighbourhood stewardship networks and tools that promote collective care. These practices strengthen social cohesion, environmental awareness and everyday participation.
digital_innovation: Digital transformation is anchored in the “Yo Alcalde” civic platform, which allows residents to report environmental issues, map local needs and collaborate on habitat maintenance. This tool facilitates transparent communication between citizens and administration, enhances participatory governance and supports the monitoring of green infrastructure across neighbourhoods.
b1_physical: Existing large tree or plant roots
c5_impacts:
Biodiversity integrated into daily urban life
Pollinator gardens, ecological corridors and micro-habitats are embedded in neighbourhood streets and parks, making biodiversity visible and tangible. This strengthens ecological resilience and expands everyday contact with nature.
Community empowerment through ecological stewardship
Residents engage in composting, community gardening and shared maintenance activities, generating a sense of collective care and reinforcing social cohesion around environmental practices.
Enhanced environmental health through diffuse green infrastructure
The widespread introduction of vegetation, permeable surfaces and shade trees improves urban microclimate, reduces heat stress and enhances the overall quality of public spaces in a tropical context.
sources:
Fiore, B. (2021, October 13). Curridabat, Costa Rica’s sweetest town. Atlas of the Future. Retrieved from https://atlasofthefuture.org/project/ciudad-dulce/
Greenfield, P. (2020, April 29). ‘Sweet City’: the Costa Rica suburb that gave citizenship to bees, plants and trees. The Guardian.

title: Grätzloasen – Neighbourhood Oases
designer: Lokale Agenda 21 Wien + participating citizen groups
promoter: City of Vienna + Lokale Agenda 21 Wien
description: Grätzloasen are small-scale green installations and parklets created by residents, neighbourhood groups and associations in collaboration with the City of Vienna. By converting parking spaces, street edges and residual areas into micro-parks with planting, benches and shading elements, the programme promotes social interaction, local identity, urban greening and heat mitigation. It is a bottom-up system enabling citizens to design, build and maintain small public spaces that improve liveability in dense neighbourhoods.
physical_innovation: The project transforms parking bays, sidewalks and small paved areas into temporary green micro-spaces equipped with planters, mobile seating, permeable surfaces and shading structures. These lightweight, modular installations reduce sealed surfaces, introduce vegetation and provide immediate improvements in comfort and walkability.
social_innovation: Grätzloasen are co-designed and co-built by residents through participatory workshops and community proposals. This model empowers neighbourhood groups, strengthens local ties, promotes collective care practices and fosters active engagement with public space. The programme provides funding, mentoring and tools to support citizen-led placemaking.
digital_innovation: The programme uses online platforms for open calls, application procedures, mapping existing parklets and sharing design guidelines. Digital tools help coordinate community groups, document processes and disseminate replicable solutions for small-scale urban greening.
c5_impacts:
Community ownership of public space
Grätzloasen strengthen social cohesion by enabling residents to collectively design, build and care for small public oases. This creates a sense of shared responsibility and enhances neighbourhood identity.
Micro-green spaces that improve liveability
By adding vegetation, shade and permeable surfaces, parklets improve comfort in highly paved areas, reduce heat accumulation and provide everyday opportunities for rest and informal social interaction.
Low-cost, scalable transformation of streets
The programme demonstrates that small, modular interventions can quickly convert parking spaces into people-centred areas, promoting walkability and preparing neighbourhoods for broader climate adaptation measures.
sources:
City of Vienna – Urban Oases: https://smartcity.wien.gv.at/en/neighborhood-oasis | Hickman, M. (2025, August 14). Why would you take away a parking place? The city where anyone can build an urban oasis. The Guardian. 
Lokale Agenda 21 Wien – Grätzloasen: https://www.graetzloase.at

title: Orto WOW
designer: OrtiAlti landscape architects and social innovation team
promoter: City of Turin and local associations (within ProGiReg / EU Horizon 2020)
description: Orto WOW transforms a former industrial complex in Mirafiori Sud, Turin, into a productive green infrastructure combining a rooftop garden, a pollinator-friendly garden, an apiary and community spaces. The project enhances biodiversity, supports urban food production, and introduces nature as a driver of social inclusion, environmental education and neighbourhood regeneration. Orto WOW is part of the EU project ProGiReg and integrates ecological restoration with community-based management.
physical_innovation: The intervention includes a rooftop garden, raised wooden beds, pollinator-friendly planting, a small apiary, permeable surfaces and micro-habitat structures. These elements reactivate a formerly sealed industrial site, increase biodiversity, create ecological corridors and provide new outdoor spaces for community use.
social_innovation: Orto WOW enables shared governance through a collaboration pact among associations, citizens, cooperatives and municipal actors. Activities include environmental education, collective gardening, workshops, volunteering, social agriculture and inclusion initiatives, reinforcing community cohesion and shared care of the space.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Ecological regeneration of a post-industrial site
The creation of a rooftop garden, pollinator beds and an apiary reintroduces biodiversity into a formerly sealed industrial area, enhancing ecological connectivity and providing habitat for pollinators.
Strengthened community cohesion through shared stewardship
Workshops, collective gardening and social agriculture activities foster collaboration between associations, residents, schools and volunteers, increasing community ownership and social inclusion.
Educational impact on urban biodiversity and food production
The garden and apiary serve as open-air classrooms, enabling children, families and citizens to learn about pollinators, soil health, urban agriculture and sustainable practices.

title: Parque Bicentenario
designer: El Equipo Mazzanti
promoter: City of Bogotá
description: Parque Bicentenario is a major urban regeneration project that reconnects two areas of Bogotá long divided by the construction of Avenida El Dorado. Built as a large elevated park spanning over a transport corridor, it integrates terraces, ramps, plazas and green spaces to restore pedestrian continuity and expand public life. The project physically “stitches” an urban wound by recreating links to the historic Parque de la Independencia while offering new areas for leisure, culture, mobility and ecological restoration.
physical_innovation: The park creates a continuous elevated landscape above an existing road trench, combining structural decking, terraced platforms, vegetated slopes, plazas, and pedestrian ramps. It reintroduces green areas over a highly artificial setting and reconnects broken urban edges through walkable infrastructure, integrating vegetation and rest areas into a complex multi-level design.
social_innovation: By bridging neighbourhoods once physically divided, the project improves accessibility to cultural institutions and creates new inclusive public spaces for walking, gathering, leisure and informal activities. The restoration of continuity to Parque de la Independencia revives historical memory and strengthens social attachment to the area, fostering renewed urban identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Reconnection of fragmented urban fabric
By bridging a major road corridor, the park restores pedestrian continuity between formerly disconnected districts, significantly improving accessibility to cultural and civic spaces.
Transformation of a traffic barrier into public space
The elevated deck turns a previously hostile road trench into a multifunctional urban park, increasing walkability, comfort and opportunities for public use in a densely built area.
Revitalization of historical green heritage
The project re-establishes continuity with Parque de la Independencia, reviving a historic landscape interrupted by mid-20th-century infrastructure and enhancing civic memory and identity.
sources: Greenroofs.com – Project page: https://www.greenroofs.com/projects/parque-bicentenario-bogota/ | ArchDaily Chile – Project article: https://www.archdaily.cl/cl/898371/parque-bicentenario-un-proyecto-que-ayuda-a-coser-una-herida-urbana-en-bogota | Metropolis Magazine – Park analysis: https://metropolismag.com/projects/el-equipo-mazzanti-bogota-bicentenario-park

title: Linear Park Ferrocarril de Cuernavaca
designer: Gaeta-Springall Arquitectos
promoter: Government of Mexico City
description: The Linear Park Ferrocarril de Cuernavaca transforms a former railway corridor into a continuous urban greenway, reconnecting neighbourhoods across Mexico City through a 4.5 km landscape strategy. The built first phase (1.4 km) integrates forest-like planting, pedestrian and cycling paths, plazas, sports areas and ecological corridors. It restores public space in dense urban areas, promotes active mobility, and creates a linear urban forest that links 22 neighbourhoods.
physical_innovation: The project converts a disused railway into a multifunctional green corridor with dense tree planting, permeable paths, sports areas, rest zones and ecological micro-habitats. It creates a linear urban forest, integrating nature with mobility, recreation and neighbourhood reconnection.
social_innovation: The park provides safe, inclusive and accessible public space in areas lacking recreational infrastructure. It encourages walking and cycling, supports everyday social life and fosters a shared sense of identity along neighbourhoods historically separated by the railway infrastructure.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Green reactivation of a disused railway corridor
Transforming an abandoned rail line into a linear urban forest improves ecological connectivity and introduces new habitat for biodiversity in a densely urbanised context.
Enhanced pedestrian and cycling mobility
The new pathway system creates a safe, continuous and shaded mobility corridor, encouraging walking and cycling while linking 22 neighbourhoods previously disconnected.
Improved urban well-being through accessible public space
New plazas, rest areas and shaded zones offer everyday outdoor opportunities for socialisation, sport and recreation, improving quality of life in areas lacking green infrastructure.

title: Îlots de fraîcheur (Isles of Coolness)
designer: City of Paris
promoter: City of Paris
description: The Îlots de fraîcheur programme is a city-wide network of cooling spots designed to help Paris cope with extreme heat. The initiative maps and enhances more than 1,400 public cool areas—including shaded parks, fountains, misting devices, permeable plazas, and naturally cooled public buildings—offering accessible refuges during heatwaves. The system creates distributed thermal comfort infrastructure integrated into urban life and the city's heatwave emergency plan (Plan Canicule).
physical_innovation: The programme introduces a distributed system of cooling infrastructures across Paris, combining vegetation, water, permeable materials and purpose-built micro-architectures. In addition to shaded parks and misting devices, several pilot îlots de fraîcheur incorporate lightweight canopy structures, refrigerated benches, and shaded seating modules equipped with evaporative cooling or integrated airflow systems. These micro-architectures create comfortable resting zones even in highly mineral urban areas, extending the cooling effect to streets, squares and schoolyards where natural shade is limited. The combination of vegetated surfaces, permeable pavements, water atomisation and engineered cooling structures forms a hybrid NBS–tech system that reduces local heat accumulation and enhances thermal comfort in dense neighbourhoods.
social_innovation: The programme prioritizes vulnerable groups (derly, children, isolated individuals) by providing a publicly accessible map of cool areas, extended opening hours of public buildings, and community awareness campaigns. The system reshapes heatwave response as a shared civic responsibility.
digital_innovation: A digital interactive map (“Paris – Où se rafraîchir ?”) displays all cooling spots in real time, including opening hours, shade levels, water points and accessibility features. The tool is integrated with the city’s heatwave alert system and mobile applications.
c5_impacts:
City-wide thermal comfort infrastructure
The programme distributes more than 1,400 cooling spots across Paris, offering shaded, vegetated and water-equipped places that significantly reduce heat stress during extreme temperatures.
Improved protection for vulnerable groups
Through targeted communication, extended opening hours of public buildings and easily accessible digital maps, the system supports elderly people, families, children and isolated residents during heatwaves.
Integration of cooling strategies into everyday urban life
By transforming parks, schoolyards and public buildings into cool refuges, Paris embeds climate adaptation into the city’s routine functioning, creating a multi-layered network of comfortable outdoor and indoor spaces.
sources: News article on pilot cool islands: https://www.leparisien.fr/paris-75/paris-teste-trois-ilots-de-fraicheur-pour-lutter-contre-les-coups-de-chaud-20-07-2018-7827912.php | City of Paris – Official cooling map: https://www.paris.fr/en/pages/where-to-cool-off-in-paris-when-it-s-hot-28052 | Plan Canicule documentation: https://cdn.paris.fr/paris/2021/05/12/92b8f55c368b25f126cd870d2343210b.pdf

title: Bankside Urban Forest
designer: Witherford Watson Mann Architects + Better Bankside Urban Design Team
promoter: Better Bankside BID and London Borough of Southwark
description: Bankside Urban Forest is a long-term urban regeneration strategy that reimagines the dense and mineral fabric of the Bankside district as a connected network of green streets, pocket parks, planted squares and pedestrian routes. Rather than creating a single forest, the project implements distributed micro-interventions—tree planting, widened sidewalks, seasonal gardens, vegetated benches and shaded civic spaces—to enhance ecological connectivity, thermal comfort, biodiversity and social life. The strategy is co-developed with local businesses, cultural institutions (such as Tate Modern) and residents.
physical_innovation: Transformation of streets and leftover spaces into a distributed system of green infrastructures: tree-lined routes, permeable surfaces, planted benches, vegetated viaducts, pocket parks and seasonal gardens. Streets are redesigned to prioritise pedestrians and create shaded microclimates, improving walkability and environmental resilience.
social_innovation: The programme is co-created with residents, businesses and cultural organisations. It supports community stewardship, activates underused areas, fosters social cohesion and creates opportunities for participation in urban greening. Public spaces become shared neighbourhood assets shaped by local identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts:
Distributed greening of a dense urban district
Through micro-interventions—tree planting, pocket parks, planted benches and seasonal vegetation—the project has significantly increased green presence in narrow streets and hardscaped areas, improving comfort and urban character.
Improved pedestrian experience and connectivity
Greener streets and widened sidewalks create safer, more attractive routes linking key landmarks (Tate Modern, Borough Market, riverfront), enhancing walkability and local economic vitality.
Strengthened community involvement and stewardship
Businesses, cultural institutions and residents actively participate in planting, maintenance and design decisions, fostering a sense of shared responsibility and long-term care for public space.
sources:
Better Bankside Urban Forest Programme: https://betterbankside.co.uk/bankside/urban-forest/
